posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked: According to an email I received, WA hired Kristaps Karklins for champagne (and Chablis) reviews. It’s not clear if Yohan Castaing will continue with his champagne reviews or if Kristaps will cover the whole. In any event, this seems to complete the handover from William to others for champagne. Somewhere, Donald is probably giddy.

posts.name: Jordan_Rock
posts.cooked:
My initial reaction is not one of excitement as I really loved William’s reviews, articles, and general perspective on Champagne. Also, I feel like not long after I understood William’s palate and to what extent it lined up with mine in the context of Champagne, I now need to calibrate a new palate/reviewer.

That being said, I know nothing about Karklins and he could be phenomenal, and having another person on the team could lead to more and more in-depth Champagne reviews/articles, so I’m withholding judgement (and crossing my fingers) for the time being.

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked: I agree with Jordan about the loss of William Kelley from Champagne. I do not share the same sensibilities with Yohan when it comes to Maisons, nor do I have confidence in his reviews, the most recent of which seem to be predicated on cost and reputation. I’m withholding judgement about Karklins, as I know nothing about his preferences or writing. I only subscribe to TWA for Champagne and will make my renewal decision accordingly.
posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: I knew this would happen when he took on Bordeaux. There are lots of competent people who do Bordeaux, Williams reviews don’t vary all that much from the herd already entrenched there. He could have much more of an impact in Champagne where things are way more interesting. Next thing you know he will drop Burgundy. When that happens I am out also …

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
I know Kristaps and I know he loves Champagne and knows it well (even if we sometimes disagree - a healthy thing!), so am excited to see what he brings to the job. I also very much enjoy William’s work, like everyone else on this forum*, but as he’s taken on the editorial role at WA it’s a sensible move to focus his reviews to two regions as he hires reviewers he has selected to focus their efforts on the other regions.

*Maybe with one exception

I can’t believe we are six posts in to a Wine Advocate post, and I get to be the one to say it:

I wonder if this will free William up to spend time integrating reviews into CellarTracker?

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: This is a dream that will not die. Wouldn’t hold my breath. WA is owned by investors. They will not want to dilute the brand.

posts.name: Mark Morr i ssette
posts.cooked:
Speaking from experience: there is nothing investors like more than “sticky” revenue.

I have tediously copied and pasted every WA review I care about on all of my wines into CT. I can unsubscribe from WA today, and still have all of those reviews.

With Vinous, I have have access to reviews on my wines in CT because they are integrated. If I unsubscribe, I can no longer see any of them.

Guess which one I am likely to stay subscribed to longer?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: We’ve gotten into this before! I am super up for it, and our ownership has no objections at this point. However, to do it would require a complete data dump of all our content, something which we are just not able/allowed to do. At such a time as we can figure out an API solution, I am all in favor of it.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: TWA is owned by Michelin.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Hi Jordan!

I think you have every reason to be optimistic! I have wanted to bring Kristaps onto the team for some time, so he is very much my hand-chosen successor for Champagne. We are tasting together regularly, both Champagnes and other wines, from bottle and barrel. So I do not think you should expect some sort of rupture or new direction, but rather deeper, broader and more timely coverage in the same spirit, taken further.

His first reports will be out before the end of the year, so you’ll have the chance to judge for yourself before too long.

posts.name: Ulrich Schäfer
posts.cooked:
I’m not here to represent Donald, and this will be my only post—I have no interest in joining the “snake pit” where people like GregK spread toxicity, and where those who challenge him, like Donald, end up banned.

As for William Kelley, while I don’t share Donald’s intense criticism, I can’t ignore a sense of disappointment. Kelley often seems overwhelmed or perhaps is simply stretched too thin. His reviews of grower champagnes are inconsistent, and it feels like he only reviews producers from whom he might have personal stakes or allocations.

Kelley’s high ratings have undeniably driven up prices for certain producers, notably Egly and Ulysse Collin. This influence has led to a sort of speculative frenzy around these champagnes, contributing to inflated prices. It’s also worth noting that French customs are now investigating alleged price-fixing among champagne producers, with Ulysse Collin reportedly under scrutiny. It will be intriguing to see where this investigation leads.

Meanwhile, the grower champagne scene itself is evolving rapidly. Many of the old guard seem to be coasting, raising prices but offering little innovation. In contrast, a new wave of producers is moving toward low-intervention or minimal-intervention winemaking, producing wines without dosage or added sulfur. These new-generation producers are often overlooked by mainstream reviews yet are selling out quickly—a testament to their appeal. Domaine Vincey is a good example: after initially receiving high scores from Kelley, they’ve shifted dramatically toward minimal intervention with each release, completely changing their approach to champagne.

This kind of valuable information about emerging trends is precisely what’s missing from Wine Advocate. I subscribed initially, hoping for deeper insights, but eventually canceled my subscription. In my view, Vinous has the strongest champagne reviewer in Anne Krebiehl MW. She doesn’t have the market-moving power of Kelley, but she offers more genuine, detailed coverage. Kelley, on the other hand, has built his profile by riding the wave of already popular producers, awarding high scores to those who were on the verge of breaking through. As he himself acknowledged in the Ulysse Collin thread, the grower champagne market is shifting—but he’s more a follower of these changes than a leader.

New Wine Advocate Champagne Reviewer WINE TALK

We are tasting together regularly, both Champagnes and other wines, from bottle and barrel. So I do not think you should expect some sort of rupture or new direction, but rather deeper, broader and more timely coverage in the same spirit, taken further. His first reports will be out before the end of the year, so you’…

(Chuckles)

When visiting friends in October I kept reiterating that I hesitated to write up more of my Burgundy trip & exploring Gevrey and Pommard because I could sense that there was someone just dying to make their first ever post

I shall be very curious to see what these newer voices contribute. I’ve only met Kristaps, but hopefully I’ll cross paths with Anne as well one day. I think Kristaps is quite smart. The more brilliant, thoughtful people we have out here in the review world, the better .
